metadata.seed: It's about the same. I'm still feeling pretty down. I mean, I just. I feel really stuck and not happy, and I just don't know why.

patient.query: It's about the same. I'm still feeling pretty down. I mean, I just. I feel really stuck and not happy, and I just don't know why.

automatic_thoughts: I don't know why I'm not happy

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and unhappy, and that can be exhausting. You’re also wondering why you can’t feel better, which adds to that sense of being trapped. Even small shifts might help, so we could explore what tiny changes might feel a bit easier to manage.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that's a good way to put it. I feel like I've been in this rut for so long, and it's frustrating. Sometimes I think about making changes, but then I just... I don't know, it's like something holds me back. Even the little things feel overwhelming.

neutral: Loss of interest in previously enjoyable activity (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve been feeling stuck and frustrated for a while, and even small tasks feel heavy. That sense of being held back can be overwhelming. What do you think might be contributing to that feeling?

patient.query: I think a lot of it is just this nagging voice in my head that tells me I'm not good enough. No matter what I try, it feels like I end up disappointing myself or others. I guess it makes me second-guess everything, and then I end up doing nothing at all. It’s just... exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I end up disappointing myself or others

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice has been weighing on you a lot, making you feel exhausted and stuck. Feeling like you’re not good enough and fearing disappointment can be really draining. You’re not alone in having those thoughts, and it’s understandable that they can lead to hesitation and inactivity.

patient.query: It’s just this nagging voice in my head that never really goes away. It tells me I’m not good enough, and no matter what I do, I feel like I end up disappointing myself or others. It makes me second-guess everything, and then I just end up doing nothing at all. It’s really exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I end up disappointing myself or others

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Tends to turn anger against self (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how exhausting that constant self‑criticism feels. What evidence do you have that you’re not good enough, and can you recall any moments when you’ve met or even exceeded your own expectations? It might help to look at those times as evidence that you’re capable of doing more than you think.

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, I get that, but it’s hard to shake those negative thoughts. I can think of a few times where I did well at something, like when I finished a project at work that I was proud of. But then, I quickly brush it aside and focus on the things I messed up or could have done better. It’s like I can never really accept any praise, even when it’s there.

llm_response.response: Do you notice any moments when you actually felt proud or accepted the praise? What evidence supports the idea that you can't accept praise, and what evidence might contradict that? How might you reframe the situation so that the praise feels more like a reflection of your effort rather than a threat to your self‑image?

patient.query: I mean, there are times when I’ve felt proud, like when my colleagues complimented me on a presentation. But then, almost immediately, I thought they were just being nice or that it was a fluke. It’s like I can't let myself believe it's genuine. I guess I do sometimes feel good for a moment, but it quickly turns into doubt. I can't really imagine how to make the praise feel less threatening... it's just so ingrained, you know?

automatic_thoughts: I can't let myself believe it's genuine

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the praise feels both exciting and unsettling at the same time. What evidence do you have that the compliments were sincere, and what evidence might suggest they were just polite? How might you view the situation if you imagined a supportive friend giving you the same feedback?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s a weird mix of feelings. On one hand, I know my colleagues wouldn’t just say nice things for no reason; they’re not like that. But then, there's this part of me that just assumes they’re being polite because I want to believe that nobody’s really that impressed. I guess if a friend told me the same thing, I’d probably be more willing to accept it. I’d think they really meant it. But when it’s about me... I just can

automatic_thoughts: I wouldn't believe it about myself

entailment: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between recognizing your colleagues’ sincerity and doubting your own worthiness. What evidence do you have that they might be offering compliments just out of politeness? And how would you feel if you let yourself accept their praise as genuine?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel stuck like that. It’s just... I’ve been told things like that before, and I always questioned if it was genuine. I mean, maybe it’s just hard to imagine that people would see something good in me. If I let myself believe their compliments, it feels risky, like I’m setting myself up for disappointment. But there’s a part of me that wishes I could just accept it without all the doubt. It would be... freeing, I think

automatic_thoughts: I always questioned if it was genuine
Maybe it’s just hard to imagine that people would see something good in me
If I let myself believe their compliments, it feels risky
I’m setting myself up for disappointment

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to feel valued and fearing that the praise might be false. What moments or details make you think the compliments might not be genuine? And what would it look like if you let yourself consider that some of those compliments could be sincere?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts about compliments being risky
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I think it’s the way I see myself sometimes—like I should be doing better or that I’m just lazy. So when someone says something nice, I immediately think they’re just being nice because they don’t see the full picture. I guess if I let myself think they might be sincere, it would mean I’d have to confront this idea that I’m not enough, and that’s scary. It’s easier to dismiss it than to really accept it. But... maybe it would feel good

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how much it feels like you’re carrying a heavy self‑critique that makes accepting praise feel risky. What kinds of moments or thoughts give you confidence that someone’s compliment is genuine? If you could try to notice one small instance where a compliment felt true, how might that shift your view of yourself?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess when someone compliments me and seems really genuine about it—like they’re making eye contact and their tone is sincere—I sometimes feel a flicker of belief. But it fades so quickly because I start thinking about all the things I should be doing instead. If I could hold onto that moment, maybe it could help me see that I’m not just fooling myself. But it’s hard to shift that mindset; I feel like I’d have to fight against a lot of old habits

automatic_thoughts: I should be doing other things
behaviors: struggle to hold onto the moment

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a brief spark of confidence when someone genuinely compliments you, but then you shift your focus to what you “should” be doing. What evidence do you have that this feeling is just a fleeting illusion? How might you view that moment differently if you allowed it to stay for a bit longer?
